State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance                Administration              Executive/Legal              Enforcement              Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662                    322-5660                    322-5901                    322-6441                  322-6444

January 22, 1981


Roy H. Mann
Clayson, Rothrock & Mann
P.O. Box 1447
Corona, CA 91720

Dear Mr. Mann:

     Thank you for your letter of November 19, 1980; I have
read it with interest. However, after careful consideration,
I cannot agree with your conclusion that disqualification
can never be required when board members vote on the type of
water tolls described in your letter. The purpose of
this letter is to explain the reasoning behind my conclusion
that disqualification may have been required under Government
Code Sections 87100 and 87103 in connection with the May 19,
1980, vote.

     The following analysis is based on the facts supplied
in your letter and in our telephone conversation of December 15,
1980. The facts are sufficient to establish that disqualification
may have been called for. However, without more details as
to the financial interests of the individual board member in
question, I cannot say whether he or she should in fact have
been disqualified.

     Water for irrigation in the Palo Verde Valley is provided
by the Palo Verde Irrigation District from a series of irrigation
canals. Most of the land in the Palo Verde Irrigation District
can obtain gravity flow water from these canals. However,
some land is too high to receive gravity flow water and
requires pumping for irrigation purposes. The district pays
for the pumping to some of this high land as part of an
agreement relating to relocation of canals; for other high
land the landowner pays for the pumping. Therefore landowners
of high land which is not provided with free pumping have
extra irrigation costs which other landowners in the district
do not have. The cost of irrigation water is established by
the Palo Verde Irrigation District Board. The Board is
authorized by Water Code App. Section 33-10a to set water
tolls for use of irrigation water within its district in one
of two ways:

Roy H. Mann
January 22, 1981
Page Two


          (1) It can set a rate based on the amount of
water used by each landowner within the district; under
this system, only landowners who actually use water for
irrigation will pay the toll; or

          (2) It can set a rate which is not based upon
water use; under this system all landowners within the
district whose land may be entitled to water for irrigation
may be required to pay the toll, whether or not they
actually use water for irrigation.

     The Board has chosen the second method of setting water
tolls. On May 19, 1980, the Board set the water tolls in
the following fashion:

          (1) For all high land for which free pumping is
not provided by the district, and for which the landowners
actually provides pumping, the toll is $22.50 per acre.

          (2) For all other land suitable for agriculture
in the district, including land irrigated by gravity
flow water, by free pumping provided by the district,
and land not irrigated or used for agriculture at all,
the toll is $27.50 per acre.

     This system represented a change from the previous
system, in which all agricultural land in the district (whether
being so used or not) was assessed a water toll at a flat
rate per acre. The new system, in effect, grants a $5 per
acre credit to landowners who pay to pump irrigation water.
This credit was intended to help offset the increased costs
of pumping for these landowners.

     The question has been raised as to whether a board
member whose land was affected by these new water toll rates
should have disqualified himself or herself from the May 19,
1980, vote on the new system of assessing water tolls.
Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103 generally require a
government official to disqualify himself or herself from
any decision which may foreseeably have a material financial
effect, on the income, investments or real property of the
official. "Material financial effect" is defined in 2 Cal.
Adm. Code Section 18702.

     In your letter you suggest that disqualification would
never be required by members of the irrigation board in
connection with the setting of water tolls, despite any
effect the tolls might have on an individual board member.

Roy H. Mann
January 22, 1981
Page Three


There are only three possible grounds for not requiring
disqualification of a board member who is materially affected
by the water toll within the meaning of 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18702. Each of those possible reasons will be discussed
below.

     (1)  This is a "uniform, ad valorem property tax rate"
or a "uniform water toll."

     The Palo Verde Irrigation District Code provided that
disqualification is not required where a vote concerns only
"a decision to fix an ad valorem property tax rate for the
District or to fix a uniform water toll."17

     The toll set by the May 19 vote is clearly not an ad valorem
property tax; it is not based on the value of the land, but
only on the number of acres and whether or not the landowner
pays for pumping.

     The toll is not a "uniform water toll" because there
are different rates for different types of land. Thus the
exemption in the Board's Conflict of Interest Code does not
appear to apply to this situation.

     (2)  The Rule of Necessity.

     Your letter suggested (indirectly) that the rule of
necessity applies here because all Board members are affected,
either beneficially or adversely, or both, because they all
must own land in the district to be members of the Board.
Water Code Appendix Sections 33-2, 33-4, 33-6.

     There are several problems with applying the rule of
necessity to this situation.  First, not all board members
necessarily own land subject to the water tolls; it appears
from the letter of the Board's counsel that there have been
occasional Board members who only owned land in the district
which was not suitable for agriculture and who, therefore,
did not pay any water tolls.

     Second, even if all board members own land which is
subject to water tolls, and thus have some financial interest
in the water rates, it does not mean they are all disqualified.
Not every financial interest in an action of the Board is
disqualifying. The interest must be of the kind specified
by Government Code Section 87103 or by the Board's Conflict
of Interest Code.  See Government Code Section 87302(b).
Under both Section 87103 and the Board's Conflict of Interest

_______________
     1/  This provision is no longer included in the new
standardized code.

Roy H. Mann
January 22, 1981
Page Four


Code disqualification is required only when the effect of a
decision is on land in which the board member has an interest
of over $1,000, or from which he receives at least $250 in
income, and only when the effect on that property is "material."
A "material" financial effect is defined in Commission regulation
2 Cal.Adm.Code Section 18702. A copy of this regulation
is attached to this letter.

Looking at these definitions, it is not necessarily
true that every water toll rate set by the Board will have a
material financial effect on the financial interests of
every Board member, despite the fact that all board members
must own land in the district.

Even if, in a particular situation, an action would
have a material financial effect on every board member, the
appropriate response would not necessarily be to allow all
members to vote. Under Government Code Section 87101, as
interpreted by the Commission, otherwise disqualified board
members can vote only if their participation in a decision
is "legally required." That is the case only if the Board
is the only body which can set water toll rates, and if the
Board cannot act without the disqualified member of members
because of a quorum requirement. If an otherwise disqualified
member's vote is necessary to break a tie, but not to constitute
a quorum, it is not "legally required" under Government Code
Section 87101, under the Commission's regulations, 2 Cal.
Adm. Code Section 18701(c), or under the Board's own Conflict
of Interest Code, and the board member cannot vote to break
the tie. If there are not enough qualified board members to
act on the water toll rate, then otherwise disqualified
members can vote under the "legally required" provision of
Government Code Section 87101. Even in such a case, only
enough otherwise disqualified members as are needed to constitute a quorum may be permitted to vote under the Commission's
interpretation of Government Code Section 87101. See In the
Matter of Hudson, 4 FPPC Opinions 13 (No. 77-007, Feb. 7,
1978).

(3) The "Public Generally" doctrine.

Under Government Code Section 87103, an official does
not have to disqualify himself from making of participating
in a decision even if the decision will have a material
financial effect on his interests, if that effect is not
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
The Commission has, by regulation, set standards for determining
under what circumstances the public generally doctrine may

Roy H. Mann
January 22, 1981
Page Five


be applied. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703. Under this
regulation, if landowners constitute the entire public, or a
significant segment of the public, of the Palo Verde Irrigation
District, the public generally exception of Government Code
Section 87103 will apply.

     The Legislature has declared that the people of California
have a "primary and supreme interest" in securing to the
"inhabitants and property owners" of the Palo Verde Valley
the greatest possible use and protection of available water
from the Colorado River. Water Code App. §33-1. To further
this interest the Palo Verde Irrigation District is established.
Only landowners in the district are permitted to vote on
setting up the Board of Trustees of the district or in electing
its members. Water Code App. §§ 33-2, 33-4, 33-6. The
number of votes each elector has is determined by the value
of his land holdings. Water Code §33-6. Only landowners
can be elected members of the Board. Water Code App. §33-5.
Water charges can only be assessed against landowners; they
alone pay the entire costs of operation of the irrigation
district. Thus, under Government Code Section 87103(c), and
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703, landowners in the Palo Verde
Irrigation District can be taken to constitute the public
generally for purposes of actions of the Board in setting
water toll rates.

     However, even if landowners do constitute the public
generally, there is still a question of whether the effect
of the action in question on the board member in question is
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally. If
so, the "public generally" exception does not apply and the
board member must disqualify himself or herself. The effect
of a decision on a particular board member is distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally if it does not affect
the board member's interest in substantially the same manner
as it affects all landowners within the district. 2 Cal.
Adm. Code Section 18703. Setting a water toll rate which is
not based on the amount of water used, and which gives a $5
per acre credit to landowners who pay for their own pumping,
does not appear to affect all landowners' interests in sub-
stantially the same manner. Therefore the public generally
doctrine does not apply in the case of this rate-setting.
(However, the public generally doctrine would apply to a
truly uniform water toll.)

     On the basis of the foregoing analysis, if a particular
decision on a water toll rate, other than a uniform ad valorem
toll, will have material financial effect on the financial

Roy H. Mann
January 22, 1981
Page Six


interests of any board member distinguishable from its effect
on the public generally, that member must disqualify himself
or herself from participating in that decision. Whether
disqualification is required in any particular instance will
depend on the nature of the decision and the particular type
of interest the board member has. Once disqualified, the
board member cannot vote to break a tie among the other
members. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18701(c). If so many
members are disqualified that there cannot be a quorum of
qualified members, a quorum of the board can be reconstituted
by the participation of otherwise disqualified members.
Hudson, 4 FPPC 13 (1978).

If you need assistance in determining whether or not
disqualification is called for in any future matter, please
feel free to contact this office for advice.

Very truly yours,

Barbara A. Milman
Staff Counsel
Legal Division

SAM:plh
Enclosure

• • • • •

CLAYSON, ROTHROCK & MANN
ATTORNEYS AT LAW
CLAYSON LAW BUILDING
601 SOUTH MAIN STREET
POST OFFICE BOX 447
CORONA CALIFORNIA 91720

FOR MORE INFORMATION:
ERLING C. AREND
DERRILL K. YAEGER
ARNOLD MANN
IVA N L. HOPKINS
JAMES L. CRAIG
DAVID M. SCIORTINO
JEAN C. MARSHALL
ELISABETH SICHEL

WALTER S. CLAYSON
(1887-1972)

E. SPURGEON ROTHROCK
(1918-1979)

AREA CODE 714
737-1910
689-7241

November 19, 1980

Ms. Barbara A. Milman, Counsel
Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

RE: Palo Verde Irrigation District
     Possible Conflict of Interest

Dear Ms. Milman:

     The President and Board of Trustees of the Palo Verde
Irrigation District asked me to respond to your letter of
October 24, 1980 with regard to a possible conflict of
interest problem in setting the 1980-1981 water tolls.

     Palo Verde Irrigation District's Act (Water Code Appendix
Section 33-10a) provides that the Board of Trustees of the
district in lieu of levying taxes may fix rates of toll and
charges for irrigation and other public uses and collect them
from all persons using water for irrigation and/or from all
persons owning or possessing land within the district which
may be entitled to water for irrigation. It provides that
if the tolls and charges are fixed and levied upon any basis
other than in accordance with the use of water, the Board of
Trustees will hold a hearing upon the manner, rate and amount
of such tolls and changes.

     Not all of the land in the Palo Verde Irrigation District
uses irrigation water, but members of the Board of Trustees
are required to be owners of real property within the district
(Water Code Appendix Section 33-5). As a result, the question
of whether or not to levy a water toll and how much of a water
toll to levy involves a conflict of interest. Under the PVID
act, all of the revenue required to operate the district could
be raised through taxes. Historically, virtually all of the
trustees have paid water tolls although there have occasionally

Ms. Barbara A. Milman
November 19, 1980
Page 2


been members of the Board who did not. Revenues of the
district have been raised primarily from water tolls and
taxes have been a minor part of the total revenues.

     Accordingly, there is a conflict of interest on whether
or not any water toll should be levied and, if a water toll
is levied, how much it should be. In addition, there is a
conflict of interest as to what land should pay water tolls
and whether or not the water tolls should be different for
different lands. Because every member of the Board of
Trustees is required to own land in the district, every
member of the Board of Trustees has a conflict of interest
on all water toll matters.

     Most of the land in the Palo Verde Valley can obtain
gravity flow water from the established canals. Some of the
land in the district is above the canals and some of the land
is so close to the level of the canals that pumping is required
to efficiently irrigate the land. This raises the cost of
irrigating those lands to which water is being pumped. In
some cases, pumping is actually necessary, in other cases it
improves efficiency.

     In the past the district has paid for pumping the water
to some lands as part of an agreement relating to relocation
of canals. In the past all lands have been charged the same
water toll, whether or not pumping was required, and whether
or not pumping was furnished by the district. Lands which
required pumping were normally assessed at lower values than
lands which did not require pumping.

     When water tolls were relatively small and the total
operational costs of the district were relatively small, the
cost of pumping was not a major item and it was at least,
in part, offset by the lower assessed value. The greatly
increased cost of energy and the fact that most of the
district's expenses are borne by the water tolls has made the
tax adjustment somewhat meaningless.

     In this context a motion was made at the March 18, 1980
meeting to give a $5.00 per acre credit against the water toll
to those lands which were pumping water which pumping was not
already paid for by the PVID. I understood the motion to be
a proposal to change the basis for levying the water toll which
could not be accomplished without following the Code Section
relating to advertising, hearing and adopting the water toll
(Water Code Appendix Section 233-10a). The PVID Conflict of

Ms. Barbara A. Milman
November 19, 1980
Page 3


Interest Code does not disqualify a trustee from voting to
"fix a uniform water toll" by reason of "investment, interest
in real property..." et cetera (Section D-2). Since the PVID
Act requires that a notice be published and a hearing be held
in the event the water toll is to be set on any basis other
than accordance with the use of water, it is necessary to
decide in advance what the basis for the water toll will be.
I, therefore, regarded the action being taken at the March 18,
1980 meeting as a part of the process to "fix a uniform water
toll" and, therefore, owning land which pays water tolls was
not a disqualifying interest.

Even in the absence of the "uniform water toll" exemption,
it would be my opinion that a trustee is not disqualified from
voting on water tolls just because he owns land that pays water
tolls. If that were true, only those persons not owning land,
and therefore not financially affected by setting the water
tolls, could vote on setting water tolls. Since every trustee
is required to own land there would be no trustee without a
conflict of interest. If some land in the district gets its
water toll reduced or removed, it pays less proportionately
and all other lands pay more proportionately, so all landowners
who pay water tolls have a financial interest in what lands pay
water tolls and how much they pay. All persons who own land
which do not pay water tolls have a similar interest.

One of the trustees, not the one who made the motion,
disqualified himself because he owns land to which water is
being pumped and which would qualify for the $5.00 reduction
in water toll. In my opinion he was not disqualified to vote
because setting the basis of the water toll is a part of the
process of fixing the water toll. Accordingly, when there was
a tie vote without him I advised him he was entitled to vote.
He voted in favor of advertising the water toll with the $5.00
reduction.

The word "uniform" was inserted in the PVID Conflicts of
Interest Code at the request of prior counsel for the Fair
Political Practices Commission, after I had explained to him
in great detail the very problems that have now arisen. In my
opinion the word "uniform" should be interpreted to mean "of
general application" and the water toll is "uniform" if it
applies equally to everyone who owns property of a particular
kind. It should be noted that some lands in the district pay
no water tolls at all. If the particular trustee were dis-
qualified from voting because he has an interest in land which
would pay a lower water toll, it would seem to follow that any

Ms. Barbara A. Milman
November 19, 1980
Page 4


trustee who owns land which pays no water toll would also be
disqualified, and a trustee which has no land which would
receive the reduced rate, but who does not pay water tolls
on other land, also has a "financial interest" in the decision.

Since the water toll cannot be set upon any basis other
than the use of water without notice of public hearing, the
motion on March 18 amounted to a proposal as to how the notice
of the fixing of the water toll should be written when it was
published.

The result of the action on March 18 at which the trustees
voted was that the advertised notice of the proposed water toll
provided that the Board of Trustees "proposed to fix the water
toll at $22.50 per acre, or fraction thereof, for all land
too high to receive gravity flow of water from the existing
canal system, and to which water is being applied by pumping
the cost of which pumping is not paid for by the district, and
$27.50 per acre, or fraction thereof, for all other lands
feasible for agriculture within the district, regardless of
whether said land is planted in crop and using water or not."
Without the motion the words "$22.50 per acre, or fraction
thereof, for all lands too high to receive gravity flow water
from the existing canal system and to which water is being
applied by pumping, the cost of which pumping is not paid for
by the district and" would not have been included in the notice
and could not have been fixed as the water toll in the absence
of another advertised notice and hearing. The vote taken on
March 18, 1980 had no immediate effect except on internal
district procedures.

The adoption of the proposed water toll was advertised
as required in the act and came up for hearing on May 19, 1980.
At that time no one appeared to protest the proposed water
toll and no written protests were received, and on May 19, 1980,
the water toll was adopted, all trustees voting in favor of it.

As I see it, the financial interests of every member of
the Board of Trustees was affected.

A trustee who owns land which paid no water tolls would
have had his tax burden greatly increased if the water toll
had not been levied. Whether or not a $5.00 difference in
water tolls between the pumped land and gravity flow land
would adversely affect him would depend on whether the burden
was passed to the gravity flow water tolls or to the taxes.

Ms. Barbara A. Milman
November 19, 1980
Page 5


     A trustee who owns gravity flow lands was directly
interested because to the extent the expenses of the district
were raised by water tolls and not by taxes, the relative
burden on gravity flow lands would be increased.

     Finally, the owner of land receiving pumped water, which
would have a reduced water toll, had a conflict because the
reduced water toll credit would be beneficial to him.

     There is also an overlying problem which involves the
relative proportion of each type of land owned by the trustee
within the district. That is, a trustee may own some of each
type of land. That was probably true in the case of the
trustee who initially disqualified himself. Although we did
not actually check on the number of acres, the result could be
that even as to a trustee who owned land to which water was
being pumped he might have enough land ownership receiving
gravity flow water that the net effect on him would be to
increase his water tolls. Whether or not this would be true
would depend on the exact proportion of each type of land which
he owns.

     As you can see, we have a rather complicated situation on
our hands and it has been my opinion that all trustees are
qualified to vote on matters affecting the water toll, because
they are all required to own land in the district. As I
indicated above, this was the topic of consideration discussion
when we were adopting the original Conflicts of Interest Code
for the district, and I sincerely hope the Fair Political
Practices Commission will bear problems like this in mind
trying to set a uniform Conflicts of Interest Code.

                                   Yours very truly,

                                   [signature]
                                   Roy H. Mann

RHM:slh
cc: Paula Todd, PVID

JOSEPH WOOLDRIDGE*
A. CAMERON PAULDEN*
JOHN B. YOUNG*
MICHAEL R. YOUNG*
ELDON R. HUGIC*
DAVID GRIFFIN*
ERNEST A. CONANT
WILLIAM C. KUHS
MICHAEL E. HUGIC
STEVE W. NICHOLS
*A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION.

LAW OFFICES

YOUNG, WOOLDRIDGE, PAULDEN, SELF, FARR & HUGIC
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING PROFESSIONAL CORPORATIONS
1675 CHESTER AVENUE
FOURTH FLOOR

BAKERSFIELD, CALIFORNIA 93301

April 22, 1981

Ms. Barbara A. Milman
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Re:  Palo Verde Irrigation District
     Your Letter of January 22, 1981
     Our File: GF 6.2B

Dear Ms. Milman:

     Mr. William Kuhs has provided us with a copy of your letter of
January 22, 1981 and his letter in response thereto, dated April 20,
1981.

     Although our interest in this matter arises from our representa-
tion of several public districts involved in distribution of water
for agricultural purposes, this letter is not written for, nor
authorized by, any of our clients. This office concurs with the
views expressed by Mr. Kuhs and we urge that his letter be taken
into consideration should further action be taken on this matter.

     In addition to the matters discussed by Mr. Kuhs, the narrow
interpretation adopted in your letter of January 22, 1981 appears to
negate landowner/director action upon even a "uniform water toll."
Tolls can only be extracted to pay district expenses. To the extent
water tolls are not established, the expenses are met from revenues
from the landowners, either raised by ad valorem or special benefit
assessments. Since it is unlikely that all land in a district is of
equal assessed value or has the same cost of service, it can be
argued that the landowners "benefit unequally" from the revenues
raised by equal water tolls. That is, the land with the highest
assessed value or at the greater elevation will pay a lesser share
of district costs if all costs are paid from a uniform water toll
(i.e., single price toll). We find no reason why a landowner/director
cannot act to establish a water toll that is to have uniform applica-
tion in accordance with the facts considered.

                                        Very truly yours,

ACP:la                                  A. C. PAULDEN

cc:  Mr. William C. Kuhs, Esq.

LAW OFFICES OF

WILLIAM C. KUHS

WILLIAM C. KUHS
JAMES R. PARKER, JR.
GAY LYNNE NATHO
REBECCA WISEMAN

1213 SEVENTEENTH STREET

BAKERSFIELD, CALIFORNIA
(805) 322-4004

April 20, 1981

Ms. Barbara A. Milman
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Re: Palo Verde Irrigation District: Advice Letter
Dated January 22, 1981

Dear Ms. Milman:

We have reviewed your letter dated January 22, 1981, in
which you conclude that a member of the Board of Trustees of Palo
Verde Irrigation District may have been disqualified under Government Code sections 87100 and 87103 relative to the decision to
establish water tolls. We do not quarrel with your primary
conclusion, however, we believe that your interpretation of
"uniform water tolls" and the "public generally doctrine" is too
narrow. Thus, the purpose of this letter is to provide you with
some background information in the event you are called upon to
provide further advice on this subject.

At the outset, let me make clear that this letter is not
written on behalf of any of our clients and the views we express
are our own. We do not represent Palo Verde Irrigation District
and we have not been requested to write this letter on its behalf.
We do represent districts in the San Joaquin Valley but we have
not been authorized or directed to write this letter on their
behalf. The sole purpose of this letter is to acquaint the
Commission with relevant facts so that if the Commission is
called upon to do so it, or its staff, may reach a proper result.

Let me first summarize some of the conclusions contained in
your letter of January 22, 1981, which cause us concern. First,
on page 3 you conclude that a water toll is not a "uniform water
toll" if there "are different rates for different types of land."

Law OFFICES OF
WILLIAM C. KUHS

April 20, 1981
Page 2

There are many factors which enter into the establishment of
water tolls¹ and we believe that a conclusion that water tolls
must be the same to be "uniform" is contrary to law. Second, on
page 4 you observe that "it is not necessarily true that every
water toll rate set by the Board will have a material financial
effect on the financial interests of every Board member . . .."
In the vast majority of landowner-voting districts, however, you
will find that every member of a board of directors will have a
financial interest in the establishment of water tolls. Third,
on page 5 you conclude that the "public generally doctrine" does
not apply where water tolls are not the same for all landowners
since the establishment of water tolls "does not appear to affect
all landowners' interests in substantially the same manner." You
note, however, that the public generally doctrine would apply to
a "truly uniform water toll." For the reasons stated below, we
believe your analysis of uniformity is too narrow.

In the southern San Joaquin Valley, two types of districts
are often organized for the distribution of water, water districts
organized under Division 13 of the Water Code and water storage
districts organized under Division 14 of the Water Code. Water
districts and water storage districts, like reclamation districts
organized under Division 15 of the Water Code, the Palo Verde
Irrigation District, and like special and general law districts,
are landowner-voting districts. As a general rule, only landowners
can vote in district elections and their votes are based on
the basis of assessed valuation. (Wat. Code, §§34026, 34027 and
35003 (water districts -one vote per dollar's worth of land);
Wat. Code, §§39020 and 41001 (water storage districts - one vote
for each one hundred dollars worth of land).) Thus, as a general
proposition, in landowner-voting districts landowership is a
qualification for office of director and voting is weighted in
proportion to value of land holdings.

In the vast majority of cases a director of a water district,
a water storage district, or a like district, will have a financial
interest in the establishment of water tolls within the meaning
of Government Code section 87100. No director may make, participate
in making, or in any way attempt to use his official position to
influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason
to know he has a financial interest. (Gov. Code, §87100.) A
director has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial

_____________

1. Water charges are oftentimes referred to as "rates,"
"tolls," or "charges." For clarity, we will use the phrase
"water tolls" to include, where appropriate, water rates, charges,
etc.

LAW OFFICES OF
WILLIAM C. KUHS

APRIL 20, 1981
Page 3

effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on any real property in which the director has an interest worth
more than $1,000. (Gov. Code, §87103, subd. (b).) In virtually
all cases a director will own land worth more than $1,000. In my
humble judgment, a battery of accountants, lawyers and other
professionals would be unable to meaningfully apply section 18702,
subdivision (b)(2), of the Regulations (Cal. Admin. Code, tit. 2,
§18702, subd. (b)(2)) in the context of the decision to establish
water tolls. Thus, pragmatically, counsel has but two alternatives:
(1) rule that the director is not disqualified or; (2) rule that
there is a disqualification and, under the rule of necessity,
requalify sufficient directors so that the board can take action.

It has been our consistent opinion that a director in a
landowner-voting district has a financial interest in the decision
to establish of water tolls and such interest will have a material
financial effect on any real property in which the director has
an interest but that such effect is not distinguishable from its
effect on the public generally so long as the water tolls are not
discriminatory. We believe this opinion to be sound and supported
by the statutory and decisional law applicable to the establishment
of water tolls. Such law, as we understand it, is briefly set
forth below.²

A water district is authorized to make water available to
landowners and "fix and collect charges therefor." (Wat. Code,
§35470.) These charges may include "standby charges" and the
charges may vary in different months and in different localities
of the district to correspond to the cost and value of the service.
(Ibid.) A water storage district may likewise fix tolls and
charges for the use of water, including the use of groundwater.
(Wat. Code, §§43006 and 47180.) These tolls and charges are to
be proportional, as nearly as practicable, to the service rendered.
(Ibid.) Because of unique circumstances, certain districts have
special legislation authorizing the levying of charges on a per
acre, zone or like basis. To our knowledge none of the water
tolls established are "uniform" in the sense that all water tolls
are exactly the same throughout the district.

In the absence of legislative direction, water tolls established
by a district must be reasonable. (See Lassen Irr. Co. v. Long
(1909) 157 Cal. 94; Leavitt v. Lassen Irr. Co. (1909) 157 Cal. 82;

___________

2. Water districts, water storage districts, and like
districts, are usually empowered to levy assessments and like
charges or water tolls or like charges based upon water use. We
do not address the statutory and decisional law relating to the
use of taxes and assessments to finance water service and restrict
our discussion to the use of water tolls, all or in part, to
finance water service.

LAW OFFICES OF
WILLIAM C. KUHS

April 20, 1981
Page 4

Kennedy v. City of Ukiah (1977) 69 Cal.App.3d 545, 552;
English Manor Corp. v. Vallejo Sanitation & Flood Control Dist.
(1974) 42 Cal.App.3d 906, 1003; Boynton v. City of Lakeport Num.
Sewer Dist. (1972) 28 Cal.App.3d 91, 97-98; 2 Wiel, Water Rights
in the Western States (3d ed. 1911) §§1247, 1249, 1279, 1283,
1317, 1319.) To be valid, water tolls must not be unjust,
unreasonable, unjustly discriminatory or unduly preferential.
(See 12 McQuillian, Municipal Corporations (rev. vol. 1970)
§34.97, p. 222.) Differentiation in water tolls charged to
customers differently situated is not in itself objectionable as
discriminatory. (Metropolitan Water Dist. v. Marquardt (1963) 59
Cal.2d 159, 186 (excess land surcharge valid); Duran v. City of
Beverly Hills (1940) 39 Cal.App.2d 133, 139 (different rates for
non-residents valid).) Water tolls below cost (e.g., Kahn v.
East Bay Mun. Util. Dist. (1974) 41 Cal.App.3d 337) or water
rates in excess of cost (cf. Metropolitan Water Dist. v. Marquardt
(1963) 59 Cal.2d 159, 182-184) appear lawful so long as the
authority to establish water tolls is not used as a general
revenue device (see City of Madera v. Black (1919) 181 Cal. 306).
Under traditional concepts, a district can probably establish
lower tolls for volume users (see 12 McQuillian, Municipal
Corporations (rev. vol. 1970) §34.101, p. 231) and under modern
concepts a district can establish higher tolls for volume users
(Williard v. Glenn-Colusa Irr. Dist. (1927) 201 Cal. 726, 174-745).
Some courts have held that different water tolls may not be
established according to the use of the water supply on the basis
of the value of the service to the customer (see 12 McQuillian,
Municipal Corporations (rev. vol. 1970) §34.101, p. 232) but
other courts have held to the contrary (see City of Fresno v.
State of California (1963) 372 U.S. 627).

Based on the foregoing, we believe that the board of a
district may lawfully consider many factors in the establishment
of water tolls, including the manner and rate of delivery, the
time of delivery, the type of water service, the cost of delivery,
the productivity of the lands, the subsurface drainage of lands,
the type of crops (cf. Wat. Code, §§35454 and 35454.5), the
volume of water used, whether the water used is within or without
the district, and like factors. In our judgment, only under the
most remote set of circumstances would the established water
rates be "uniform" throughout the district.3

In our judgment a director does not have a financial interest
in the decision to establish water tolls which is distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally if the board of directors

3. The word "uniform" means "not varying or variable"
(Websters New Collegiate Dict. (1976) p. 1279) and its synonyms
include equal, even, constant, unvaried, invariable, unvarying,
consistent and alike (Roget's International Thesaurus (2d ed.
(1962)) §17.5, p. 8).

LAW OFFICES OF

WILLIAM C. KUHS

April 20, 1981
Page 5

of a district establishes reasonable classifications for different
water tolls and the water tolls are uniform within each classification.
Stated somewhat differently, if the water tolls are lawfully
established in the first instance, by definitive the effect of
the decision on the director is indistinguishable from the effect
on the public generally. In the context of your letter of
January 22, 1981, if the Board of Trustees of Palo Verde Irrigation
District established reasonable and nondiscriminatory water tolls
through its action of May 19, 1980, none of the trustees was
disqualified.

We understand that your letter of January 22, 1981, does not
constitute an opinion of the Commission or a declaration of
policy by the Commission (Cal. Admin. Code, tit. 2, §18329,
subd. (g)) and that the effect thereof, accordingly, is limited.
We intend to counsel our clients as we have in the past, namely
that a director is not disqualified from making a decision to
establish lawful water tolls. If you or the Commission has an
opportunity to consider this subject further, we ask that you
consider the views contained herein before any action is taken.

In closing, we thank the Commission for providing us with
your letter of January 22, 1981, and Roy H. Mann, Esquire's letter
of November 19, 1980.

Very truly yours,

William C. Kuhs
William C. Kuhs

WCK:nlo
cc: Roy H. Mann, Esq.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether trustees of the Palo Verde Irrigation District have disqualifying conflicts of interest when voting on water toll matters, given that all trustees are required to own property in the district and would be financially affected by toll decisions.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document appears to be incoming request/correspondence rather than FPPC advice letter response. No formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections present. Document is truncated at end. Two separate letters visible - November 1980 and April 1981. Extracted information from November 1980 letter which contains the substantive analysis.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Whether trustees of the Palo Verde Irrigation District have disqualifying conflicts of interest when voting on water toll matters, given that all trustees are required to own property in the district and would be financially affected by toll decisions.
embedding.first_500_words: State of California Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance Administration Executive/Legal Enforcement Statements of Economic Interest (916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444 January 22, 1981 Roy H. Mann Clayson, Rothrock & Mann P.O. Box 1447 Corona, CA 91720 Dear Mr. Mann: Thank you for your letter of November 19, 1980; I have read it with interest. However, after careful consideration, I cannot agree with your conclusion that disqualification can never be required when board members vote on the type of water tolls described in your letter. The purpose of this letter is to explain the reasoning behind my conclusion that disqualification may have been required under Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103 in connection with the May 19, 1980, vote. The following analysis is based on the facts supplied in your letter and in our telephone conversation of December 15, 1980. The facts are sufficient to establish that disqualification may have been called for. However, without more details as to the financial interests of the individual board member in question, I cannot say whether he or she should in fact have been disqualified. Water for irrigation in the Palo Verde Valley is provided by the Palo Verde Irrigation District from a series of irrigation canals. Most of the land in the Palo Verde Irrigation District can obtain gravity flow water from these canals. However, some land is too high to receive gravity flow water and requires pumping for irrigation purposes. The district pays for the pumping to some of this high land as part of an agreement relating to relocation of canals; for other high land the landowner pays for the pumping. Therefore landowners of high land which is not provided with free pumping have extra irrigation costs which other landowners in the district do not have. The cost of irrigation water is established by the Palo Verde Irrigation District Board. The Board is authorized by Water Code App. Section 33-10a to set water tolls for use of irrigation water within its district in one of two ways: Roy H. Mann January 22, 1981 Page Two (1) It can set a rate based on the amount of water used by each landowner within the district; under this system, only landowners who actually use water for irrigation will pay the toll; or (2) It can set a rate which is not based upon water use; under this system all landowners within the district whose land may be entitled to water for irrigation may be required to pay the toll, whether or not they actually use water for irrigation. The Board has chosen the second method of setting water tolls. On May 19, 1980, the Board set the water tolls in the following fashion: (1) For all high land for which free pumping is not provided by the district, and for which the landowners actually provides pumping, the toll is $22.50 per acre. (2) For all other
embedding.summary: This is correspondence from attorneys representing the Palo Verde Irrigation District to FPPC counsel regarding potential conflicts of interest in setting water tolls. The attorneys argue that all board trustees have conflicts but are not disqualified from voting because the statute exempts "uniform" water tolls and because every trustee is required to own land in the district.
